description: Adding time-range analytics on a 2B-row events table — review index strategy and ALTER semantics on a non-partitioned hot table
review_checklist: Evaluate BTREE vs BRIN for time-correlated columns on huge tables
Check ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN with non-IMMUTABLE/non-constant DEFAULT semantics
Identify need for partitioning before adding indexes
Verify CONCURRENTLY usage on production-scale indexes

ground_truth.description: CREATE INDEX without CONCURRENTLY on a 2B-row table acquires SHARE lock on the entire table for the full build duration (estimated 6-12 hours). All writes block. Use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY.

BTREE index on occurred_at is a poor choice for an append-only time-series table at 2B rows. Use BRIN (Block Range INdex) — it's ~1000x smaller, supports range scans efficiently on naturally-ordered data, and has near-zero write overhead. For events table the index would shrink from ~60GB to ~50MB.

Composite index (event_type, occurred_at) is created but no partitioning exists. With 80M new rows/day and unbounded growth, this index will become the dominant cost on every INSERT and bloat unboundedly. The schema requires PARTITION BY RANGE (occurred_at) before any time-based indexing strategy makes sense.

ADD COLUMN with non-IMMUTABLE DEFAULT (EXTRACT depends on a value derived from another column) forces a full table rewrite — PG cannot use the fast metadata-only path introduced in PG 11. On 2B rows this rewrite is multi-day with AccessExclusiveLock. Use a separate UPDATE batch job or a generated column.

Adding a denormalized cohort_bucket column duplicates information already in occurred_at. Use a GENERATED ALWAYS AS (...) STORED column or compute at query time — denormalization on a 2B-row table costs ~16GB and risks staleness if the formula changes.

VACUUM ANALYZE inside the migration script blocks until full statistics gather completes. On 2B rows this can run hours. Let autovacuum handle this asynchronously, or use ANALYZE-only (skip the vacuum).
